title: Bacterial Meningitis
objectives: Recognize clinical features of bacterial meningitis
Apply the classic triad and meningeal signs
Select appropriate empiric antibiotics by age/risk

vignette.text:
A 22-year-old college student is brought to the ED confused and difficult to arouse. He complained of severe headache and neck stiffness last night, with multiple episodes of vomiting. He lives in a dormitory and recently had an upper respiratory infection.

Past Medical History: None, immunizations up to date including meningococcal vaccine

Medications: None

Social History: College junior, dormitory, no drugs/alcohol

Allergies: None

physicalExam.neck: Marked nuchal rigidity, positive Kernig and Brudzinski signs
physicalExam.skin: Petechial rash on trunk and extremities
physicalExam.neurologic: GCS 12, oriented to person only, no focal deficits

questions.stem: Meningitis is inflammation of the meninges.
questions.question: What are the three layers of the meninges?
questions.hint: CSF flows in the subarachnoid space.

options.text: Dura mater, arachnoid mater, pia mater (outer to inner)
Pia mater, arachnoid mater, dura mater

questions.explanation: The meninges from outside to inside: Dura mater (tough outer layer), Arachnoid mater (web-like middle), Pia mater (delicate inner layer on brain surface). CSF circulates in the subarachnoid space between arachnoid and pia.

sources.text: OpenStax A&P 2e, Chapter 13
StatPearls: Meninges

questions.stem: Lumbar puncture is performed to obtain CSF.
questions.question: At what vertebral level is LP performed and why?
questions.hint: The spinal cord ends at a specific level.

options.text: L3-L4 or L4-L5; spinal cord ends at L1-L2, avoiding cord injury

questions.explanation: LP is performed at L3-L4 or L4-L5, below the conus medullaris (spinal cord ends at L1-L2 in adults). The cauda equina floats in this area and moves away from the needle.

sources.text: StatPearls: Lumbar Puncture
OpenStax A&P 2e

questions.stem: He has nuchal rigidity and positive meningeal signs.
questions.question: What is the anatomical basis for nuchal rigidity?
questions.hint: The meninges extend around the spinal cord.

options.text: Inflamed spinal meninges cause pain and spasm when stretched by neck flexion

questions.explanation: The meninges surround brain and spinal cord. Inflammation makes them painful when stretched. Neck flexion stretches cervical meninges, triggering pain and reflex muscle spasm (rigidity).

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.
StatPearls: Bacterial Meningitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: CSF opening pressure is 32 cm H2O (elevated).
questions.question: What causes elevated ICP in bacterial meningitis?

options.text: Cerebral edema, impaired CSF absorption, inflammatory exudate

questions.explanation: Elevated ICP results from: vasogenic edema (BBB disruption), cytotoxic edema (cellular injury), impaired CSF absorption at arachnoid granulations, and inflammatory exudate. ICP elevation contributes to morbidity.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.
StatPearls: Bacterial Meningitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: CSF glucose is 28 mg/dL with serum 95 (ratio 0.29).
questions.question: Why is CSF glucose low in bacterial meningitis?

options.text: Bacteria and neutrophils consume glucose; impaired transport across inflamed BBB

questions.explanation: CSF glucose is low due to consumption by bacteria and neutrophils (anaerobic glycolysis) and impaired transport across inflamed blood-CSF barrier. Ratio <0.4 suggests bacterial meningitis. His is 0.29.

sources.text: StatPearls: CSF Analysis
IDSA Guidelines

questions.stem: CSF protein is 245 mg/dL (elevated).
questions.question: Why is CSF protein elevated?

options.text: BBB breakdown allows serum proteins to leak into CSF

questions.explanation: CSF protein is elevated due to BBB disruption allowing serum proteins (albumin, Ig) to leak into CSF. Normal is 15-45 mg/dL; his 245 indicates significant inflammation.

sources.text: OpenStax Anatomy and Physiology 2e. OpenStax, Rice University, 2022. Accessed January 14, 2026.
StatPearls: Bacterial Meningitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: CSF shows 2,400 WBC with 95% neutrophils.
questions.question: What does neutrophilic pleocytosis indicate?
questions.hint: Different infections have different CSF profiles.

options.text: Bacterial infection - neutrophils predominate; viral typically shows lymphocytes

questions.explanation: Bacterial meningitis: high WBC (>1000), neutrophil predominance. Viral: lower WBC (50-500), lymphocyte predominance. TB/fungal: lymphocytes, very low glucose. His profile (2400 cells, 95% PMNs) is classic bacterial.

sources.text: IDSA Meningitis Guidelines
StatPearls: CSF Analysis

questions.stem: Gram stain shows gram-positive diplococci.
questions.question: What organism is most likely causing his meningitis?
questions.hint: Consider epidemiology and Gram stain appearance.

options.text: Streptococcus pneumoniae - most common cause in adults

questions.explanation: Gram-positive diplococci in CSF = S. pneumoniae (pneumococcus). It's the most common cause of bacterial meningitis in adults. N. meningitidis is gram-negative diplococci. The petechial rash is concerning but can occur with any severe bacterial infection.

sources.text: IDSA Meningitis Guidelines
StatPearls: Pneumococcal Meningitis

questions.stem: He has a petechial rash.
questions.question: A petechial/purpuric rash in meningitis raises concern for which organism?
questions.hint: One organism is classically associated with this finding.
questions.explanation: Petechial/purpuric rash is classic for meningococcemia (N. meningitidis), though can occur with any sepsis/DIC. Meningococcal disease can cause fulminant purpura fulminans. His Gram stain shows gram-positive diplococci (pneumococcus), but empiric coverage should include meningococcus given rash.

questions.stem: He recently had an upper respiratory infection.
questions.question: How does URI predispose to bacterial meningitis?

options.text: Viral URI disrupts mucosal barrier, facilitating invasion by colonizing bacteria

questions.explanation: S. pneumoniae and N. meningitidis colonize the nasopharynx. Viral URI damages the mucosal barrier, allowing bacteria to invade blood and cross the BBB. This is why meningitis often follows URI.

sources.text: StatPearls: Bacterial Meningitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Tunkel AR, et al. Practice Guidelines for the Management of Bacterial Meningitis. Clin Infect Dis. 2004;39(9):1267-1284. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: He was vaccinated against meningococcus.
questions.question: Why might a vaccinated individual still get meningococcal disease?

options.text: Vaccines cover specific serogroups; serogroup B requires separate vaccine

questions.explanation: Quadrivalent meningococcal vaccine covers serogroups A, C, W, Y. Serogroup B (common in college outbreaks) requires a separate MenB vaccine. Also, vaccine immunity may wane. His Gram stain suggests pneumococcus anyway.

questions.stem: Empiric antibiotics must be started immediately.
questions.question: What is the empiric antibiotic regimen for a young adult with suspected bacterial meningitis?
questions.hint: Cover the most common pathogens for his age group.

options.text: Ceftriaxone + Vancomycin (add ampicillin if Listeria risk)

questions.explanation: Empiric regimen for 2-50 years: Ceftriaxone 2g IV q12h (covers pneumococcus, meningococcus, H. flu) + Vancomycin (for penicillin-resistant pneumococcus). Add Ampicillin for age >50, immunocompromised, or alcoholics (Listeria coverage).

sources.text: IDSA Meningitis Guidelines 2017
StatPearls: Bacterial Meningitis

questions.stem: Dexamethasone is being considered.
questions.question: What is the indication and timing for dexamethasone in bacterial meningitis?

options.text: Give BEFORE or WITH first antibiotic dose for suspected bacterial meningitis; reduces mortality in pneumococcal meningitis

questions.explanation: Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV q6h × 4 days. Must give BEFORE or WITH first antibiotic dose to reduce inflammatory response to bacterial lysis. Proven benefit for pneumococcal meningitis (reduced mortality, hearing loss). Give empirically if bacterial meningitis suspected.

sources.text: StatPearls: Bacterial Meningitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Tunkel AR, et al. Practice Guidelines for the Management of Bacterial Meningitis. Clin Infect Dis. 2004;39(9):1267-1284. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Cultures confirm S. pneumoniae.
questions.question: When can antibiotics be narrowed based on culture results?

options.text: Narrow immediately based on Gram stain
Change to oral antibiotics after 24 hours
When susceptibilities return - if penicillin-susceptible, can use ceftriaxone alone; drop vancomycin

questions.explanation: Once susceptibilities confirm penicillin-susceptible pneumococcus, can narrow to ceftriaxone monotherapy. Penicillin-resistant strains need continued vancomycin. De-escalation is important for antibiotic stewardship once culture data available.

sources.text: IDSA Guidelines
StatPearls: Antibiotic Stewardship

questions.stem: Treatment duration must be determined.
questions.question: What is the typical antibiotic duration for pneumococcal meningitis?
questions.explanation: Duration by organism: S. pneumoniae 10-14 days, N. meningitidis 7 days, H. influenzae 7 days, Listeria 21 days, GBS 14-21 days. Gram-negative bacilli may need 21 days. Clinical response guides therapy.

sources.text: IDSA Meningitis Guidelines
StatPearls: Bacterial Meningitis

questions.stem: Recognizing the classic presentation is critical.
questions.question: What is the classic triad of bacterial meningitis?
questions.hint: Three cardinal features, though not always present.

options.text: Fever, nuchal rigidity, altered mental status

questions.explanation: Classic triad: fever + nuchal rigidity + altered mental status. However, only 44% have all three. >95% have at least 2 of: headache, fever, neck stiffness, altered mental status. Maintain high suspicion even with incomplete presentation.

sources.text: StatPearls: Bacterial Meningitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Tunkel AR, et al. Practice Guidelines for the Management of Bacterial Meningitis. Clin Infect Dis. 2004;39(9):1267-1284. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You're considering whether to perform LP before CT.
questions.question: When is CT head required BEFORE lumbar puncture?
questions.hint: LP can cause herniation if ICP is elevated from mass effect.

options.text: Immunocompromised, CNS disease history, new seizure, papilledema, focal deficits, or GCS <10
CT is never needed before LP

questions.explanation: CT before LP if: immunocompromised, history of CNS disease, new-onset seizure, papilledema, focal neurologic deficits, altered consciousness (GCS <10). These suggest mass lesion/elevated ICP with herniation risk. Do NOT delay antibiotics for CT - give empirically then do LP after.

questions.stem: Time to antibiotics is critical.
questions.question: What is the timing priority for antibiotics in suspected bacterial meningitis?

options.text: Give antibiotics immediately - do NOT delay for CT or LP; mortality increases with delay

questions.explanation: Give antibiotics (and dexamethasone) IMMEDIATELY when bacterial meningitis is suspected. Each hour of delay increases mortality. If CT needed, give antibiotics BEFORE CT. LP can be done after, though CSF culture sensitivity decreases after 4-6 hours of antibiotics.

sources.text: StatPearls: Bacterial Meningitis. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Close contacts are at risk.
questions.question: What prophylaxis is indicated for close contacts of meningococcal meningitis?

options.text: Rifampin, ciprofloxacin, or ceftriaxone for household/close contacts

questions.explanation: Chemoprophylaxis for N. meningitidis close contacts: Rifampin 600mg PO BID × 2 days, OR Ciprofloxacin 500mg × 1 dose, OR Ceftriaxone 250mg IM × 1 dose. Give within 24 hours of diagnosis. Close contacts = household, daycare, anyone with direct secretion exposure. Report to public health.

sources.text: CDC Meningitis Prevention
IDSA Guidelines

questions.stem: He develops a generalized tonic-clonic seizure.
questions.question: How should seizures in bacterial meningitis be managed?

options.text: IV benzodiazepines (lorazepam) acutely; consider prophylactic antiepileptic if high risk

questions.explanation: Seizures occur in 20-30% of bacterial meningitis. Treat acutely with lorazepam 4mg IV. If recurrent, load with fosphenytoin or levetiracetam. Prophylactic AEDs are debated but often given in first 48-72 hours if high risk (altered consciousness, focal deficits).

sources.text: IDSA Guidelines
StatPearls: Meningitis Complications

questions.stem: Repeat LP on day 3 shows persistent CSF abnormalities.
questions.question: What complication should be suspected with persistent CSF abnormalities?

options.text: Subdural empyema, brain abscess, or ventriculitis

questions.explanation: Lack of improvement by 48-72 hours suggests: resistant organism, inadequate antibiotic penetration, or complication (subdural empyema, brain abscess, ventriculitis, infected thrombus). Repeat imaging (MRI preferred) and consider broadening antibiotics.

questions.stem: After recovery, he has hearing difficulties.
questions.question: What is the most common permanent sequela of bacterial meningitis?

options.text: Sensorineural hearing loss (due to cochlear damage)

questions.explanation: Hearing loss is the most common sequela (10-30%), especially with pneumococcal meningitis. The cochlea is vulnerable to inflammation spreading from the meninges. All patients should have audiologic evaluation. Dexamethasone reduces hearing loss incidence.

questions.stem: Integrating all clinical information.
questions.question: Which statement BEST synthesizes this patient's presentation?

options.text: Pneumococcal meningitis in young adult presenting with classic triad, CSF showing bacterial pattern, requiring urgent ceftriaxone/vancomycin/dexamethasone

questions.explanation: This synthesis captures: organism (gram-positive diplococci = pneumococcus), presentation (classic triad, meningeal signs), CSF pattern (high WBC/neutrophils, low glucose, high protein, elevated pressure), and treatment (ceftriaxone + vancomycin + dexamethasone immediately).

questions.stem: Planning comprehensive care.
questions.question: What is the comprehensive management approach?

options.text: Immediate antibiotics + dexamethasone, ICU monitoring, ICP management, seizure precautions, contact prophylaxis notification

questions.explanation: Comprehensive plan: (1) Immediate ceftriaxone + vancomycin + dexamethasone; (2) ICU admission for monitoring; (3) ICP management (head elevation, consider mannitol); (4) Seizure precautions/treatment; (5) Public health notification for contact prophylaxis; (6) Hearing evaluation before discharge.

sources.text: IDSA Meningitis Guidelines
StatPearls: Meningitis Management

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected meningitis?

options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.
Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in meningitis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.
correct.deeperWhy: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in meningitis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in meningitis. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of meningitis. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.
While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.
Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.
correct.deeperWhy: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
correct.clinicalPearl: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

mcqs.stem: When managing meningitis, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
correct.deeperWhy: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

sections.title: Clinical Approach
sections.content: Systematic approach to Meningitis including recognition, diagnosis, and management.

imaging_reasoning.order_reasoning: CT before LP only if: altered mental status, immunocompromised, papilledema, focal neuro deficit, seizure
imaging_reasoning.expected_findings: CT usually normal in uncomplicated meningitis; MRI shows leptomeningeal enhancement
imaging_reasoning.management_impact: Don't delay antibiotics for imaging; CT clears for safe LP; MRI shows complications (abscess, ventriculitis)
